Threat Title: Cross Site Scripting
Description: The web server 'Admin Portal' could be a subject to a cross-site scripting attack because it does not sanitize untrusted input.
Justification: Apply output encoding, CSP policies, and input validation to prevent script execution.

Threat Title: Admin Portal Process Memory Tampered
Description: If Admin Portal is given access to memory, such as shared memory or pointers, or is given the ability to control what API Backend executes (for example, passing back a function pointer.), then Admin Portal can tamper with API Backend. Consider if the function could work with less access to memory, such as passing data rather than pointers. Copy in data provided, and then validate it.
Justification: Apply layered security controls and monitoring to reduce likelihood and impact.

Threat Title: Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability for HR Database
Description: SQL injection is an attack in which malicious code is inserted into strings that are later passed to an instance of SQL Server for parsing and execution. Any procedure that constructs SQL statements should be reviewed for injection vulnerabilities because SQL Server will execute all syntactically valid queries that it receives. Even parameterized data can be manipulated by a skilled and determined attacker.
Justification: Prevent via parameterized queries, strict input validation, and database least-privilege policies.

Threat Title: Spoofing the Admin External Entity
Description: Admin may be spoofed by an attacker and this may lead to unauthorized access to Admin Portal. Consider using a standard authentication mechanism to identify the external entity.
Justification: Apply layered security controls and monitoring to reduce likelihood and impact.

Threat Title: Spoofing the Payment Provider External Entity
Description: Payment Provider may be spoofed by an attacker and this may lead to unauthorized access to API Backend. Consider using a standard authentication mechanism to identify the external entity.
Justification: Payment processing risk is partially transferred to certified third-party provider under contractual agreement.

Threat Title: Potential Data Repudiation by Admin Portal
Description: Admin Portal claims that it did not receive data from a source outside the trust boundary. Consider using logging or auditing to record the source, time, and summary of the received data.
Justification: Apply layered security controls and monitoring to reduce likelihood and impact.

Threat Title: Potential Data Repudiation by API Backend
Description: API Backend claims that it did not receive data from a source outside the trust boundary. Consider using logging or auditing to record the source, time, and summary of the received data.
Justification: Payment processing risk is partially transferred to certified third-party provider under contractual agreement.

Threat Title: Data Flow SQL Query Is Potentially Interrupted
Description: An external agent interrupts data flowing across a trust boundary in either direction.
Justification: Prevent via parameterized queries, strict input validation, and database least-privilege policies.

Threat Title: Potential Process Crash or Stop for Admin Portal
Description: Admin Portal crashes, halts, stops or runs slowly; in all cases violating an availability metric.
Justification: Residual DoS risk remains despite rate limiting and WAF protections; complete elimination is impractical.

Threat Title: Data Flow Admin Login (HTTPS) Is Potentially Interrupted
Description: An external agent interrupts data flowing across a trust boundary in either direction.
Justification: Residual DoS risk remains despite rate limiting and WAF protections; complete elimination is impractical.

Description: API Backend crashes, halts, stops or runs slowly; in all cases violating an availability metric.
Justification: Payment processing risk is partially transferred to certified third-party provider under contractual agreement.

Threat Title: Data Flow Payment Webhook Is Potentially Interrupted
Description: An external agent interrupts data flowing across a trust boundary in either direction.
Justification: Payment processing risk is partially transferred to certified third-party provider under contractual agreement.

Threat Title: Cross Site Request Forgery
Description: Cross-site request forgery (CSRF or XSRF) is a type of attack in which an attacker forces a user's browser to make a forged request to a vulnerable site by exploiting an existing trust relationship between the browser and the vulnerable web site.  In a simple scenario, a user is logged in to web site A using a cookie as a credential.  The other browses to web site B.  Web site B returns a page with a hidden form that posts to web site A.  Since the browser will carry the user's cookie to web site A, web site B now can take any action on web site A, for example, adding an admin to an account.  The attack can be used to exploit any requests that the browser automatically authenticates, e.g. by session cookie, integrated authentication, IP whitelisting,   The attack can be carried out in many ways such as by luring the victim to a site under control of the attacker, getting the user to click a link in a phishing email, or hacking a reputable web site that the victim will visit. The issue can only be resolved on the server side by requiring that all authenticated state-changing requests include an additional piece of secret payload (canary or CSRF token) which is known only to the legitimate web site and the browser and which is protected in transit through SSL/TLS. See the Forgery Protection property on the flow stencil for a list of mitigations.
Justification: Enforce strong RBAC, token validation, and privilege checks at the API Gateway and Backend.

Justification: Enforce strong RBAC, token validation, and privilege checks at the API Gateway and Backend.
